did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study utilized the SkinIO mobile application, which employs a deep neural network-based outlier detection model to analyze full-body photographs and highlight suspicious lesions for triage purposes. Although final diagnoses were made by a physician, the AI system was integral to the study's workflow for identifying lesions during the teledermatology screening process.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and describes a pilot programme involving original data collection from 27 residents at a senior living community between November 2020 and July 2021. It reports specific clinical outcomes (e.g., three skin cancers detected) and patient satisfaction survey results, which constitutes original experimental/observational research rather than a review of prior literature.

title: Partnering with a senior living community to optimise teledermatology via full body skin screening during the COVID‐19 pandemic: A pilot programme
brief_summary: This pilot programme partnered with a senior living community to optimize teledermatology access during the COVID-19 pandemic by combining in-residence full-body imaging with real-time outlier lesion identification. The study utilized the SkinIO mobile application, which employs a deep neural network-based model to analyze patient images and highlight suspicious lesions for triage purposes. Non-physician clinical staff obtained high-quality full-body and dermoscopic photographs on-site for 27 residents, who were then reviewed by a dermatologist via video visit. The main evaluation approach included assessing clinical outcomes, such as the detection of skin cancers, and measuring patient satisfaction through surveys. Key results indicated that three skin cancers were identified among the participants, and 88% of patients reported satisfaction with the programme, finding it safer or comparable to in-person visits. The study concludes that this hybrid skin scan framework offers high utility for overcoming barriers to dermatologic care in vulnerable elderly populations.

all_task_types.target: Suspicious, outlier lesions for triage purposes
all_task_types.details: The paper describes the use of a deep neural network-based outlier detection model within the SkinIO application. The model analyzes full-body photographs to highlight lesions that are 'most different from the average' (outlier detection) to assist in triage. The paper explicitly states that the neural network aspect was 'only used in triage for photography, but not for diagnosis' and that 'final diagnoses were made by the physician.' Therefore, the AI task is outlier detection for triage, not classification or regression of diagnostic labels.

datasets_used.name: Stanford Skin Scan Programme Cohort
datasets_used.description: A pilot programme cohort consisting of 27 residents from the Sequoias Portola Valley Senior Living Retirement Community in Portola Valley, California, who underwent full-body skin screening and teledermatology consultation between November 2020 and July 2021.
classes_used: Skin cancer (Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma)

per_class.Benign lesions: Majority of 186 dermoscopically imaged lesions (specific count not reported)
per_class.Other conditions: Listed but specific counts not reported for individual conditions

use.other: The cohort was used for a quality improvement pilot programme to assess clinical outcomes (detection of skin cancers) and patient satisfaction with a teledermatology workflow. The SkinIO application's deep neural network was used for outlier detection during image collection, but the study explicitly states it was not used for diagnosis, and no model training or validation metrics were reported for this specific dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the study used descriptive statistics and frequency analysis.

SkinIO (Deep Neural Network-based outlie.note: The SkinIO application was used for triage and image collection assistance. The paper explicitly states that the neural network aspect was 'only used in triage for photography, but not for diagnosis' and 'final diagnoses were made by the physician'. No diagnostic accuracy metrics (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, AUC) were reported for this specific cohort's data. The paper references a previous study by Guido et al. showing SkinIO had 92% sensitivity in detecting new or changing lesions, but this is not a result obtained in the current study's dataset.

Stanford Skin Scan Programme Cohort.notes:
Clinical Outcomes:
- Total participants: 27
- Skin cancers identified: 3 (2 basal cell carcinomas, 1 squamous cell carcinoma)
- Actinic keratoses identified: 13
- Other conditions treated: Allergic contact dermatitis, androgenetic alopecia, eczematous dermatitis, groin intertrigo, heel fissure, hyperkeratosis of heels, seborrhoeic dermatitis, shingles, skin tears, stasis dermatitis.
- Individuals scheduled for follow-up in-person visit: 11
- Individuals starting home treatment: 4
- Attrition rate: 0%

Lesion Flagging:
- Total dermoscopically imaged lesions: 186
- Flagged by SkinIO application: 63% (186 lesions)
- Flagged by patient: 33% (97 lesions)
- Flagged by physician: 4% (12 lesions)

Patient Satisfaction (Survey Results, n=26 response rate 96%):
- Satisfied with Skin Scan programme: 88%
- Likely to recommend programme: 77%
- Photographs representative of skin: 92%
- Process safer or comparable to in-person visit (in context of COVID-19): 100%
- Comprehensiveness/quality comparable or better than in-person visit: 83%
- Ability to connect with dermatologist comparable or better than in-person visit: 81%
- Full body skin imaging safer or comparable to in-person visit: 100%
- Video visit safer or comparable to in-person visit: 96%
- Programme convenient, trustworthy, and representative: 92%
- Satisfied with full body imaging: 88%
- Satisfied with video visit experience: 71%
- Would prefer to see dermatologist in-person after pandemic: 31%
- Would not have sought care without the programme: 38%

Provider Survey (n=1):
- Treating dermatologist was overall very satisfied, trusted the quality of skin assessment, and felt providing care was safer than in-person visit.
- Felt quality and comprehensiveness of photographs were superior to patient-provided photographs in traditional video visits.
- Neutral about offering programme to all patients after pandemic, citing in-person visit felt more reliable.

architecture_used.main_model: SkinIO deep neural network-based outlier detection model

architecture_details.mechanism: Analyzes all photos from a patient's total body photography session, compares each lesion, and highlights lesions most different from the average
architecture_details.usage_scope: Used only in triage for photography to suggest suspicious lesions for dermoscopic photos; not used for diagnosis

training_details.details: The study was a pilot programme using the application as a tool; no model training or validation metrics were reported for this specific cohort. The paper references a previous study by Guido et al. showing SkinIO had 92% sensitivity in detecting new or changing lesions, but this is not a result obtained in the current study's dataset.

classification_or_output_head.details: Real-time flagging of lesions most different from the average to assist non-physician staff in triaging which lesions to take dermoscopic photos of

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SkinIO deep neural network-based outlier detection model
conclusions: The Stanford Skin Scan Programme offers a framework for how a hybrid skin scan programme may provide high utility for individuals with barriers to accessing in-person clinics.

The programme successfully overcame barriers to care for elderly patients in senior living communities during the COVID-19 pandemic by combining in-residence full body imaging with real-time outlier lesion identification and virtual teledermatology.

The hybrid model advances traditional teledermatology by combining store-and-forward and live-interactive models, utilizing high-quality images captured through a neural network-based application to enable synchronous screening.

The programme achieved high patient satisfaction, with 88% of patients satisfied with the programme and 100% feeling the process was safer or comparable to an in-person visit in the context of the pandemic.

The programme facilitated the detection of skin cancers (three identified) and other conditions, demonstrating its potential to maintain the continuum of dermatologic care for patients at highest risk.

The study acknowledges that the objective was not to assess the diagnostic efficacy of the SkinIO application, as final diagnoses and clinical decision-making were made by a dermatologist, not the AI system.
While patients appreciated the programme, 31% reported they would prefer to see a dermatologist in-person after the pandemic, and the treating provider felt in-person visits remained more reliable.
important_remarks: The study is a single-site pilot programme with a small sample size (n=27), limiting statistical power and generalizability.

The cohort exhibits significant demographic bias: 96% white, high median household income ($220,000), and English-speaking, which may not represent broader elderly populations facing financial or language barriers.

The study explicitly states its objective was not to assess the diagnostic efficacy of the SkinIO application; final diagnoses were made solely by a physician, and no diagnostic accuracy metrics (e.g., sensitivity, specificity) were reported for this cohort.
The treating dermatologist was a single provider who volunteered, introducing potential selection bias and limiting the generalizability of provider satisfaction results.

Patient satisfaction data is subject to volunteer bias, as participants were a self-selected group comfortable with skin photography, potentially underrepresenting those with social, religious, or privacy concerns.

The programme required significantly more resources than traditional visits (35 min clinical staff time, 20 min provider time, plus hardware costs), raising questions about cost-effectiveness and scalability for the general dermatologic population.
Technical limitations include difficulty capturing certain anatomical areas (scalp, groin) accurately with full-body photography, requiring trained staff and appropriate lighting.
The study was conducted during the COVID-19 pandemic, meaning the high perceived safety (100%) and utility may be context-specific and not directly comparable to non-pandemic settings.

While 88% of patients were satisfied, 31% preferred in-person visits post-pandemic, and the provider remained neutral about widespread adoption, citing reliability concerns compared to in-person exams.
